title: The Place | Get Assistance Today
text:
Support.Empower.Transform.
To assist the people of Forsyth and Dawson County areas with essential needs and to be self-sustaining.
Who We Are
The Place is a local nonprofit serving Forsyth and Dawson counties, helping individuals and families meet essential needs such as food, clothing, and financial assistance while guiding them toward long-term self-sufficiency through education, workforce training, and holistic support.
Values
Compassion and Hope
We provide an inclusive and welcoming environment for all clients. We review each case on an individual basis and work to address all needs we identify.
Dignity and Respect
We evaluate all processes to focus on the dignity of those we serve. We demonstrate respect by listening to and offering support to our clients.
Accountability and Stewardship
We maintain the highest standards in the nonprofit and business world. We ensure all donations are used appropriately to accomplish the greatest good. We focus on sustainability and carrying out our mission with increased efficiency and effectiveness.
Collaboration and Coordination
We bring resources together to transform lives in our area. We seek to identify gaps of needs in our community and, through collaboration, find creative ways to meet the needs.
Our Services
Food Pantry
ThriftStore
Financial Assistance
Employment Services and Adult Education
Senior Outreach
Youth Programs
Why People Trust The Place
92¢ of every $1
Goes directly into programs
450+ Families
Served each week through our food pantries
50+ Years
Serving Forsyth and Dawson counties
Partnerships
Courts, schools, and local leaders
How You Can Help
Host a drive
One-time urgent need donations
Sponsor a program
Become a Heart of Impact Donor
Subscribe for News and Events
Sign up with your email address to receive news and updates.

title: Food Pantry | Support Your Community Today — The Place
text:
Need Help With Food?
New and Existing Clients
New Clients
Emergency Food Boxes
Food support is available to anyone in need -- no appointment required. Each package includes enough food to help for several days while we connect you with a longer-term solution. Visit us during our office hours, and our team will make sure you receive what you need.

Mobile Food Pantries
The Place provides free food to anyone in need through our mobile food pantries. Our truck and trailer visit several locations throughout Forsyth and Dawson counties on a regular schedule. See below for upcoming dates and locations.

title: Financial Assistance | Get Help Today — Apply Now — The Place
text:
Short Term Financial Assistance
Request Assistance
The Place offers financial assistance for rent, utilities, and prescription costs. If The Place is able to provide financial assistance, it is typically offered as a partial payment toward the expense.
REQUIRED DOCUMENTS:
Photo ID
Photo ID
Current, signed lease
Current, signed lease
Utility bills (electric, water, gas)
Utility bills (electric, water, gas)
Proof of income
Proof of income
Current 30 days of financial transactions or bank statement
Current 30 days of financial transactions or bank statement
All requests take a minimum of three to five business days. Please note The Place does not offer ongoing financial assistance. For additional resources or if the primary residence is not within Forsyth or Dawson County, please call the United Way at 211 (toll free) for an agency that serves your area.

title: New Volunteers | Get Involved Today — The Place
text:
Become a Volunteer
Thank you for sharing your time, talent, and passion with us. Volunteers are essential to our mission of assisting the people of Forsyth and Dawson counties with essential needs and to be self-sustaining.
We couldn’t do it without you!
Types of Volunteer Work Available
In-Home Special Projects great for the whole family
In-Home Special Projects great for the whole family
Sorting and hanging clothes, shoes, accessories and home goods in Thrift Store
Sorting and hanging clothes, shoes, accessories and home goods in Thrift Store
Merchandising and organizing at The Thrift Store
Merchandising and organizing at The Thrift Store
Sorting, organizing and packaging nonperishable food items in Food Pantry or Warehouse
Sorting, organizing and packaging nonperishable food items in Food Pantry or Warehouse
Mentoring (young adult women, financial literacy, case management, etc)
Mentoring (young adult women, financial literacy, case management, etc)
Picking up bread and meat donations from local stores to bring back to The Place
Picking up bread and meat donations from local stores to bring back to The Place
After signing up you can explore additional opportunities that participants with limited mobility can do.

Please note:
Minimum age to volunteer on-site at our Thrift Store is 18 years old.
Minimum age to volunteer on-site at our Thrift Store is 18 years old.
Minimum age to volunteer on-site at our Food Warehouse/Mobile Food Pantries is 14 years old.
Minimum age to volunteer on-site at our Food Warehouse/Mobile Food Pantries is 14 years old.
LOCATIONS AND HOURS
Forsyth location2550 The Place Circle, Cumming, GA 30040Monday through Saturday from 9:00am–4:00pm
Dawson location829 GA 9 North, Dawsonville, GA 30534Closed MondaysTuesday through Saturday from 9:00am–4:00pm
What to Wear and Bring
Closed-toed shoes, comfortable clothing, no tank tops and minimal jewelry. For your safety, sandals and flip flops cannot be worn in our warehouses. REQUIRES standing, bending and lifting items up to 25lbs.
If you would like to bring a lunch or a snack, we have a refrigerator and break room. Please bring water.
Please complete the application below. You will receive an email from our Volunteer Coordinator with next steps.
Interested in Group Volunteer Opportunities?
We provide group volunteer opportunities if your school, business, or church is looking to give back. The opportunities range from clothes sorting at our Thrift Store, shopping and packing non-perishables for client orders, or possibly even something a little different! If you or your group is interested, please email our Volunteer Coordinator, Amber Pham (amber@theplacega.org)
Ongoing Volunteer Opportunities
Thrift Store Volunteers at The Place thrift store work closely with the staff unloading donations, sorting and inspecting, hanging clothes, or organizing the retail floor. This is a great opportunity for individuals and groups to make a difference. Recurring and one-time volunteer opportunities are available.
Food PantryVolunteers at The Place Food Pantry are assisting in making sure our clients are receiving all of their orders when they arrive. We provide a high level of customer service by providing car side service. You might help to pack food bags, sort through baked goods, or help load groceries into vehicles. Individual opportunities available at the food pantry and group opportunities available at the warehouse or for a food drive.
Workforce DevelopmentVolunteers looking to assist with our workforce program can help by becoming a mentor/tutor for our financial literacy class, can offer to supply a meal for any of our classes, or provide childcare services for the families that need to bring children with them. Individual and group opportunities available.
Seasonal/ Holiday The Holidays bring a great deal of volunteer opportunities around The Place. These offerings include: Thanksgiving food drive and food pantry serving, Holiday House Event, Gift of Prom, Back to School Backpacks, Hot meals for seniors on the Holidays and much more.
Home Projects The opportunities to volunteer at home are great for families with younger children that want to serve The Place. These can include: Hosting a coat drive, putting together baskets for the homebound seniors for holidays, collecting food for a food drive, and so much more!

title: April Newsletter for The Place — The Place
text:
April Newsletter for The Place
Orange Duffel Bag Initiative Graduation:Celebrating 17 Students
This past weekend, we celebrated an incredible milestone! 17 students graduated from our Orange Duffel Bag Initiative (ODBI)!
Each student stood up and gave a final presentation, sharing their goals, what they’ve learned, and the steps they are taking toward their future. It was a powerful moment to see their growth, confidence, and determination on full display.Through the 12 weeks of ODBI, students build life skills like goal setting, decision making, and healthy coping strategies. They are challenged to think about their future and take real steps toward it. At graduation, you can truly see that transformation.
Each graduate received a certificate, an orange duffel bag, and a laptop to support their next steps in school, work, and life.These moments are more than a celebration, they are a reminder of what is possible when students are supported, encouraged, and equipped with the tools they need to succeed.
Thank you to everyone who helps make programs like ODBI possible. Because of your support, these 16 students are stepping into their future with confidence and a plan.A special thank you to Hargrove and Browns Bridge Church's Be Rich campaign for sponsoring this year's class!
Want to learn more about Young Adult Programs?In this episode of The Place Podcast, Nicole sits down with Nathan Rodriguez, Young Adult Coordinator, to talk about how The Place is walking alongside students and young adults in our community.
Nathan shares about three key programs that are helping shape brighter futures:
Community School at East Forsyth High School – meeting students where they are by connecting them to resources, support, and encouragement right on campus
Community School at East Forsyth High School – meeting students where they are by connecting them to resources, support, and encouragement right on campus
Orange Duffel Bag Initiative (ODBI) – equipping students with confidence, goal-setting skills, and tools to navigate life’s challenges
Orange Duffel Bag Initiative (ODBI) – equipping students with confidence, goal-setting skills, and tools to navigate life’s challenges
Summer Internship Program – providing hands-on experience that prepares young adults for the workforce and what comes next
Summer Internship Program – providing hands-on experience that prepares young adults for the workforce and what comes next
Throughout the conversation, Nathan shares how these programs are not just meeting immediate needs, they are helping students build a foundation for long-term success and self-sufficiency.This episode is a powerful reminder that when we invest in young people, we are investing in the future of our community.Want to be part of this impact?You can support programs like these by giving, volunteering, or sharing about The Place with others.Visit ThePlaceGA.org to learn more.
Did you miss a previous episode? You can find them linked below!

Now Accepting Applications: Summer Internship Program
We’re excited to share that The Place is now accepting applications for our 6-week Summer Internship Program, running June 16 through July 23.This opportunity is designed for high school students (ages 16+) who are ready to gain real-world experience while building confidence and important life skills. Interns will explore different areas of The Place, develop job readiness skills like resume building and interview etiquette, and grow in communication, leadership, and teamwork.It’s more than an internship, it’s a chance to learn, grow, and take meaningful steps toward the future.To learn more or apply, contact Nathan Rodriguez at nathan@theplacega.org
This week, we had the pleasure of hosting our Volunteer Appreciation event!Our volunteers are the lifeline of the work we do, and we could not serve our community without them.A huge thank you to our volunteers, committee members, staff, and sponsors who made this event possible!
Events benefiting The Place
Get ready for a fantastic day on the greens at the NoFo Invitational on Monday, May 4, 2026, benefiting The Place.
Held at Polo Golf & Country Club with check‑in at 9 AM and a 10 AM shotgun start, this shamble‑format tournament includes a post‑round buffet and reception for all players.Registration is now open, $200 per player, $800 per foursome, or $1,000 for a corporate sponsorship, making it a great way to play, network, and support three impactful local organizations.
Needs and More
Join Hearts of Impact, Give Hope Every Month!
Hearts of Impact is our monthly giving community. When you join, you help The Place be there for families in Forsyth and Dawson counties, not just once, but all year long.
Monthly gifts help provide food, housing support, and hope when neighbors need it most. Because these gifts are steady and reliable, we can plan ahead, respond faster, and serve more people when needs rise.
Your monthly support means families don’t have to wait for help.
Join Hearts of Impact today and make a difference every single month.

title: March Newsletter for The Place — The Place
text:
March Newsletter for The Place
Gift of Prom reached an exciting milestone this year as we served 98 students across Forsyth and Dawson counties - our largest number ever.
What began seven years ago as a small effort to ensure that every student can experience the joy of prom has grown significantly, from serving just 23 students in 2022 to 98 in 2026. This year’s record participation reflects both the growing need in our community and the generosity of our donors and volunteers who make this event possible.
Thanks to remarkable community support, our boutique was stocked more abundantly than ever.
This year we received donations of 254 dresses, 52 tuxedos and men’s formalwear pieces, 103 pairs of shoes, 118 pieces of jewelry, and 136 makeup items. Combined with our existing inventory, we began the event with over 500 dresses, giving every student a wide range of options and styles to choose from.The abundance also allowed us to share resources with partner agencies, including Blessed Hope, Epic Prom, and White County.
The heart of Gift of Prom is always found in the personal moments, and this year brought many. One volunteer, Charlene, described her experience as “the most rewarding” she has had, sharing how meaningful it was to help teens find outfits that made them smile with pride.Charlene also recounted a touching side story: after talking about Gift of Prom with a cashier she sees regularly at Publix, the cashier, Tandy, decided to volunteer as well, and the two ended up joyfully working the same shift. These small connections reflect the spirit of community that keeps the program growing.
Another volunteer, Jean from the Forsyth County Junior Service League, shared the story of a student who had never worn a dress to a dance before.After exploring several jewel-toned options and laughing with her mom through the process, the student found a black dress that made her feel confident and beautiful. Even then, she hesitated, worried about taking something someone else might want.Jean encouraged her to choose what made her feel special, reminding her that she was the focus of the day. The student ultimately selected the dress along with jewelry and a vibrant red purse, leaving with a smile that Jean described as absolutely unforgettable.
These stories, paired with the dedication of volunteers, logistics team, staff, and the extraordinary generosity of donors, are what make Gift of Prom so impactful. Beyond providing dresses and suits, the program gives students memories of being celebrated, valued, and supported.Thank you to everyone who contributed time, resources, or care to make this year the most successful Gift of Prom yet.Your support truly changes lives and creates lasting moments these students will carry with them far beyond prom night.
In this episode of The Place Podcast, host Nicole Morgan sits down with Paige Ashkeboussi, Adult Education Coordinator at The Place. Paige shares how Adult Education programs—from GED and ESL to Financial Literacy, Parenting classes, and Summer Study Hall—help adults build confidence, stability, and brighter futures.We explore how The Place removes barriers by offering free meals, childcare, transportation, and all class materials so learners can focus on what matters most. Paige highlights inspiring stories, strong community partnerships, and the dedicated volunteers who make these programs truly life‑changing.Whether you’re interested in joining a class, supporting these initiatives, or simply learning more about how education transforms lives, this episode will leave you feeling inspired and encouraged.For questions about Adult Education programs or to get involved, contact Paige Ashkeboussi:pashkeboussi@theplacega.org770‑887‑1098 ext. 223Visit theplacega.org to explore upcoming classes, volunteer opportunities, or ways to support these impactful programs.

We’re excited to share that The Place was recently featured on The American Dream TV, an Emmy-nominated, nationally syndicated program that highlights uplifting stories about community, lifestyle, and homeownership.The show shines a spotlight on the people and organizations making a difference. Our team had the opportunity to be interviewed by local Coldwell Banker realtor Jessica Diaz, where we shared how The Place is supporting and empowering families across our community.

Our upcoming Charity Poker Tournament is your chance to enjoy an electrifying night of friendly competition, big wins, and even bigger impact. Whether you're a seasoned player or just love the thrill of the game, this event is the perfect blend of excitement and community spirit.Spots fill up fast every year, so preregister now to secure your seat at the table and help support the meaningful work happening at The Place.Your best hand might just change a life—don’t miss it!

title: February Newsletter for The Place — The Place
text:
February Newsletter for The Place
What does real, lasting change look like?
In our newest podcast episode, we sit down with Meghan Livingston, our Case Management Manager at The Place, to talk about our Long-Term Case Management Program. This powerful program is made possible through a state grant and the generous support of community donors and partners like you.
Through this program, we walk closely with families for an extended period of time as they work toward self-sustainability. Participants commit to meeting one-on-one with staff and attending adult education classes, including financial literacy classes. Together, we create a plan for stability. Along the way, we can help with rent, deposits, utilities, and other urgent expenses, removing barriers while families build the skills and confidence they need for long-term success.
This is not just short-term assistance. This is relationship-based support that helps families stay housed, grow stronger, and move forward with hope.
Right now, you can help make this life-changing work possible through our Have a Heart for Our Community matching gift campaign through the Community Foundation for Northeast Georgia. Every gift brings us closer to unlocking our matching grant to increase your impact. Your support helps keep neighbors in homes while they work toward a stable future.
Will you have a heart for our community today?
Give now and help families build lasting stability.

title: Summer Hunger Is Real. Together, We Can Make a Difference — The Place
text:
Summer Hunger Is Real. Together, We Can Make a Difference
Summer is a season of sunshine, family vacations, and children enjoying a well-deserved break from school. But for many families in our community, summer also brings a difficult reality: when school is out, access to regular, nutritious meals becomes much harder.
For many children, school breakfast and lunch programs provide consistent, healthy meals during the school year. Children can miss up to 10 meals per week during the summer months. When those meals are no longer available, parents and caregivers are left trying to stretch already limited grocery budgets to cover additional meals every day.
At The Place, we see this need firsthand. Throughout the summer, more families turn to our pantry for help because they simply need a little extra support to keep healthy food on the table.
Every child deserves a summer filled with fun, friendships, and opportunities to grow - not the uncertainty of wondering where their next meal will come from.
That's why our mission doesn't take a summer vacation.
Every week, generous donors, dedicated volunteers, and caring community partners help us provide food to children, families, seniors, and neighbors facing food insecurity. Their kindness allows us to offer hope along with every bag of groceries distributed.
But we can't do it alone.
You Can Help Feed Kids This Summer
Whether you're making your first donation or have supported our pantry for years, your generosity makes a meaningful difference.
Here are a few ways you can help:
Donate healthy, unopened, and non-expired food items that children and families can enjoy throughout the summer. Check our current food pantry needs list for what we currently need most!
Donate healthy, unopened, and non-expired food items that children and families can enjoy throughout the summer. Check our current food pantry needs list for what we currently need most!
Make a financial gift. Monetary donations allow us to purchase fresh produce, dairy products, protein, and other essential foods where they're needed most.
Make a financial gift. Monetary donations allow us to purchase fresh produce, dairy products, protein, and other essential foods where they're needed most.
Volunteer your time. From sorting donations to stocking shelves and assisting families, every volunteer helps us serve more neighbors with dignity and care.
Volunteer your time. From sorting donations to stocking shelves and assisting families, every volunteer helps us serve more neighbors with dignity and care.
Spread the word. Share our mission with friends, family, coworkers, and your social networks. You can find us on Instagram, Facebook, YouTube, and TikTok.
Spread the word. Share our mission with friends, family, coworkers, and your social networks. You can find us on Instagram, Facebook, YouTube, and TikTok.
Every Meal Matters
A meal is more than food on a plate. It gives a child energy to play, learn, and grow. It offers parents relief during financially challenging times. It reminds families that their community cares.
This summer, you can help ensure that no child misses a meal simply because school is out.
Thank you for supporting The Place and helping us support families all summer long.
Because hunger doesn't take summer break. And neither do we.

title: Caring for our Homebound Seniors: Supporting Comfort, Dignity, and Connection — The Place
text:
Caring for our Homebound Seniors: Supporting Comfort, Dignity, and Connection
As our loved one’s age, many seniors face challenges that make it difficult to leave home independently. Illness, mobility limitations, chronic conditions, or recovery from surgery can all contribute to someone becoming homebound. While remaining at home often provides comfort and familiarity, it can also create feelings of isolation and make daily tasks more difficult.
Caring for homebound seniors requires a strong support system. This is where The Place can provide some much-neededconnection they need.
The Importance of Social Connection
One of the greatest challenges homebound seniors face is loneliness. Limited mobility can make it harder to stay connected with family, friends, and the community. Regular visits and phone calls can help seniors feel valued and less isolated. The Place offers an opportunity for connection with calls, food delivery, home repairs, and visits.
Creating a Safe and Comfortable Environment
Safety is a key part of caring for seniors at home. Making small adjustments can help prevent falls and injuries, such as:
Removing tripping hazards
Removing tripping hazards
Installing grab bars in bathrooms
Installing grab bars in bathrooms
Improving lighting throughout the home
Improving lighting throughout the home
Repairing loose or rotted handrails
Repairing loose or rotted handrails
Checking and installing new smoke detectors
Checking and installing new smoke detectors
The Place provides homebound seniors with services like home repairs, installing ramps, replacing broken windows, and much more to ensure they are safe in their own home.
How Volunteers Can Help
The Place is only able to offer these services to seniors because of the help of volunteers and community partners. Volunteers help by delivering meals, making phone calls, working on home projects, and more. To learn more about how you can help support the seniors in our community, please visit the Senior Assistance page.

title: Top 10 Most Requested Pantry Items — The Place
text:
Top 10 Most Requested Pantry Items
At The Place, our food pantry is a vital resource for families across Forsyth and Dawson Counties. Food donations play a key role in helping us serve our community and keep our shelves stocked with essentials.
We’re often asked what items are most needed, so we’ve created a list of our Top 10 most requested pantry items to help guide your generous donations.
Tuna/chicken pouches. These are a great source of protein. Unlike canned goods, pouches don’t require a can opener, which makes them more accessible for someone who may not have access to a kitchen. They’re also lightweight and easier to store.
Tuna/chicken pouches. These are a great source of protein. Unlike canned goods, pouches don’t require a can opener, which makes them more accessible for someone who may not have access to a kitchen. They’re also lightweight and easier to store.
Protein shakes and bars. Protein shakes and bars are an excellent source of protein while combining convenience and nutrition.
Protein shakes and bars. Protein shakes and bars are an excellent source of protein while combining convenience and nutrition.
Flour/sugar/cooking oil/salt/pepper (pantry staples). These pantry staples are always requested by clients as they are essential in cooking and preparing meals.
Flour/sugar/cooking oil/salt/pepper (pantry staples). These pantry staples are always requested by clients as they are essential in cooking and preparing meals.
Hygiene items. Hygiene items such as shampoo, toilet paper, body wash, and laundry detergent are often overlooked as pantry staples, but these items are always in need.
Hygiene items. Hygiene items such as shampoo, toilet paper, body wash, and laundry detergent are often overlooked as pantry staples, but these items are always in need.
Canned vegetables. Canned vegetables are nutritious and do not require refrigeration, which makes it a great pantry staple.
Canned vegetables. Canned vegetables are nutritious and do not require refrigeration, which makes it a great pantry staple.
Baby food. Babies have specific dietary needs, so donating items like formula and baby food help fill that gap for caregivers.
Baby food. Babies have specific dietary needs, so donating items like formula and baby food help fill that gap for caregivers.
Canned beans. Beans are highly nutritious and packed with protein, fiber, iron and other essential nutrients making them a great meat substitute as well.
Canned beans. Beans are highly nutritious and packed with protein, fiber, iron and other essential nutrients making them a great meat substitute as well.
Rice. Rice is a staple in almost every pantry. It has a long shelf life and works well for many types of cuisines making this a popular pantry item.
Rice. Rice is a staple in almost every pantry. It has a long shelf life and works well for many types of cuisines making this a popular pantry item.
Hamburger Helper. Hamburger Helper is a great way to stretch protein and can turn limited ingredients into a complete meal.
Hamburger Helper. Hamburger Helper is a great way to stretch protein and can turn limited ingredients into a complete meal.
Mac and Cheese cups. Perfectly portioned, single-serve mac and cheese cups are convenient and are great for people who may not have access to a full kitchen.
Mac and Cheese cups. Perfectly portioned, single-serve mac and cheese cups are convenient and are great for people who may not have access to a full kitchen.
While our food pantry is grateful for every donation, here are a few important guidelines to keep in mind:
Opened food items cannot be accepted. For safety reasons, anything that has been opened must be discarded and cannot be distributed.
Opened food items cannot be accepted. For safety reasons, anything that has been opened must be discarded and cannot be distributed.
Please avoid items with damaged packaging or significant dents, as these may not be safe to provide.
Please avoid items with damaged packaging or significant dents, as these may not be safe to provide.
While it may seem obvious, we kindly ask that you do not donate expired food items.
While it may seem obvious, we kindly ask that you do not donate expired food items.

title: Youth Programs | Empower Youth Today—Join Us — The Place
text:
Helping Our Kids Succeed
Youth Programs
School Snack Packs
Through our ongoing partnerships with school social workers and staff, we consistently hear that students who are hungry have a harder time focusing, participating, and staying engaged. Hunger affects attention, energy levels, and even behavior—especially later in the school day. School social workers have identified student snacks as one of the greatest unmet needs right now. Having access to reliable, nutritious snacks helps students stay regulated, attentive, and ready to learn, ensuring they can focus on what matters most: their education.
Holiday House
Holiday House is a heartwarming program that gives parents and guardians the dignity and joy of selecting new, unwrapped gifts their children truly want. Families facing hardships are invited to shop for their kids, choosing items that fit their needs and wishes.
Orange Duffel Bag Initiative
The Orange Duffel Bag Initiative is a 12-week program that empowers young people to identify their strengths, discover their purpose, set meaningful goals, and create a plan for their future through leadership development, self-discovery, and life-skills education.
Gift of Prom
Prom is a milestone that every student deserves the chance to enjoy. Through Gift of Prom, The Place helps under-resourced students participate in this special event by providing many of the items that can make prom financially out of reach. From dresses, shoes, and jewelry to tuxedo rentals, hair and makeup services, and other essentials, Gift of Prom helps remove barriers so students can celebrate this memorable occasion with confidence.
Graduation Gifts for McKinney-Vento Students
We close out the school year on an incredible high note, celebrating our McKinney‑Vento students with graduation gifts made possible through community generosity. The McKinney‑Vento Homeless Assistance Act ensures that children and youth experiencing homelessness have immediate school enrollment, educational stability, and transportation — removing barriers that often stand between them and academic success.
Summer Internships
The Summer Internship Program provides high school students with hands-on experience in community service and nonprofit work while helping them develop leadership, professional, and career-readiness skills through workshops, tours, projects, and volunteer experiences.

title: Planned Giving | Empower Giving Today — The Place
text:
Planned Giving
Your donation helps families and individuals in our community.
Ways to Plan Your Support to The Place
Monthly Giving
By joining Hearts of Impact, you become an integral part of our efforts to foster a thriving, resilient community. Together, we can make a lasting difference in the lives of our neighbors. Click this link to join now!
Stocks, Bonds, Mutual Funds
Do you love giving and need a great tax deduction? Increase your gift to The Place with one simple strategy for valuable financial benefits. Your donation can make a larger impact by donating long-term appreciated securities, including stocks, bonds, and mutual funds directly to The Place. Utilize one of the most tax-smart ways to give and reduce future capital gains through donating these assets directly to The Place. For more information, please contact Nicole Morgan at nicole@theplacega.org
In-Kind Donations
Donations can be made in-person at our admin office located at 2550 The Place Cir. We also have an Amazon Wishlist at this link that is updated with our current needs.
Tribute Gifts
Are you looking for an impactful way to support The Place’s mission? Tribute gifts help you honor a special loved one or leave a lasting memory. This giving option also provides a great way to commemorate a joyous occasion or say ‘Thank You’ to a business client. With your gift, your recipient will receive a personalized note from The Place sharing about your tribute gift. We look forward to partnering with you on this wonderful and meaningful donation opportunity. For more information about tribute gifts, please contact Nicole Morgan at nicole@theplacega.org
IRA Distributions
Are you 70 ½ or older and looking for an IRA distribution option that is tax-free and helps your community? By donating funds directly to The Place, you can meet your required minimum IRA distribution with no taxes. This option can potentially give donors larger giving capacity than contributing cash or other assets. IRA distributions are a great way to support The Place. Would you like to engage with The Place through IRA distributions? Please contact Nicole Morgan at nicole@theplacega.org
Employee Matching Gifts
Does your company have an employee charitable matching program? Employee Matching Gifts are an easy way to double or triple your donation. Matching gift programs show employees that their company not only encourages charitable giving but is committed to making their donations go further. Enter your company’s name in the Employee Match tool to see if your company participates.
Donor Advised Funds
Looking for a way to simplify your giving to The Place? Do you regularly give to The Place? You might benefit from streamlining your giving through a donor advised fund. A donor advised fund (DAF) gives an immediate tax deduction along with potentially reducing capital gains taxes and estate taxes. You get to recommend grants from the fund over time. You can contribute as often as you like. DAF’s provide donors with a flexible way to give to The Place and a tax-savvy method for setting aside philanthropic giving. Are you interested in contributing to The Place through a DAF? Contact Nicole Morgan at nicole@theplacega.org

title: History — The Place
text:
Our History
The Place was founded in 1975 by four Dominican Nuns. The sisters offered assistance with financial needs, clothing, shelter, food, and even tutoring. Throughout our history, we have been the catalyst for several agencies that had their beginnings as programs at The Place.
In 2015, we started a workforce development initiative to help individuals who are unemployed or under-employed prepare for and secure jobs.
In 2021, we merged with Dawson County’s RIC-Rack thrift store and food pantry. We also implemented many of our current programs and services including financial assistance, workforce development and more.
Today, we serve our communities with emergency financial assistance, clothing, food, educational opportunities, plus numerous support services. Through our many initiatives and services, we are changing lives in Forsyth and Dawson counties of Georgia.
